subtopic_detail: STEMI ECG criteria - posterior MI recognition
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with nausea and diaphoresis. He has a history of hypertension and is an ex-smoker. He took two of his wife's GTN sprays without relief. The nearest primary PCI centre is 45 minutes away by road. Vital signs show HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% on room air, RR 18. He is in distress but mentating well. The initial 12-lead ECG shows 1.5 mm ST depression in V1 to V3 with prominent R waves in V2, upright T waves in V1 to V3, and 1 mm ST depression in II, III and aVF. There is no anterior ST elevation. A right-sided and posterior lead ECG is performed and shows 1 mm ST elevation in V7, V8 and V9. High-sensitivity troponin returns at 320 ng/L (reference <14). He has no contraindications to fibrinolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1.5 mm V1 to V3, dominant R in V2, upright T V1 to V3. Posterior leads V7 to V9 show 1 mm ST elevation. Inferior leads show 1 mm depression.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 320 ng/L (<14). FBC, UEC, glucose, lipids pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Conservative medical management with aspirin, ticagrelor and enoxaparin only, with serial troponins
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI is a STEMI equivalent and mandates emergent reperfusion under the Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 ACS guideline; managing as NSTE-ACS misses the time-critical reperfusion window.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Transfer for primary PCI without giving fibrinolysis, accepting the 45 minute transfer delay plus catheter-lab activation time, with full anticoagulation en route
options.rationale: Heart Foundation/CSANZ guidance: primary PCI is preferred only when first medical contact to balloon is achievable within 90 minutes (120 minutes if transfer needed); door-in-door-out plus 45 minute transport plus catheter lab activation will exceed 120 minutes, so fibrinolysis followed by transfer is indicated.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now, plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin, then transfer for pharmaco-invasive PCI within 3 to 24 hours
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI confirmed by V7 to V9 ST elevation is a STEMI equivalent requiring immediate reperfusion. When primary PCI cannot occur within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway is fibrinolysis (tenecteplase weight-banded), antiplatelets (aspirin + clopidogrel preferred with lysis), and anticoagulation (enoxaparin), followed by transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours regardless of clinical course.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Treat as NSTE-ACS, administer aspirin, ticagrelor, fondaparinux and refer for angiography within 72 hours
options.rationale: The 72 hour invasive window applies to NSTE-ACS, not STEMI or STEMI equivalents; posterior STEMI requires immediate reperfusion (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Repeat ECG in 30 minutes and reassess; if persistent changes, then consider fibrinolysis
options.rationale: The diagnosis is already established by posterior lead ST elevation and elevated troponin; deliberate delay increases myocardial loss (time is muscle) (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Posterior STEMI presents with ST depression and tall R waves in V1 to V3 and is confirmed with posterior leads V7 to V9 showing >=0.5 mm ST elevation. It is a STEMI equivalent. When primary PCI is not achievable within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway (fibrinolysis then transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours) is indicated.
key_points: Posterior STEMI = ST depression V1 to V3 with dominant R, confirmed by V7 to V9 elevation >=0.5 mm
STEMI equivalents (posterior, de Winter, Wellens evolution, LBBB with Sgarbossa) need urgent reperfusion
Primary PCI within 120 min FMC-to-balloon; otherwise lyse then transfer 3 to 24 h
Clopidogrel (not ticagrelor) is preferred with fibrinolysis to limit bleeding
explanation.pearls: Always do posterior and right-sided leads when you see V1 to V3 ST depression with upright T waves and dominant R in V2 (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: NSTE-ACS - timing of invasive strategy and GRACE risk
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to a tertiary metropolitan emergency department with three episodes of central chest pain over the last 12 hours, each lasting around 15 minutes, the last occurring 90 minutes ago at rest while she was reading on her couch. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%), longstanding hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline stable), and was admitted 18 months ago with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction managed conservatively at that admission. Her current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg. She is an ex-smoker (quit 8 years ago, 25 pack-years prior) and has a sister with CABG at 64. Vital signs at triage are HR 88 regular, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% on room air, RR 18, afebrile. She is mildly distressed but well perfused with warm peripheries. Examination is unremarkable apart from a grade 2 soft systolic murmur at the apex without radiation. The 12-lead ECG shows 1 mm horizontal ST depression in V4 to V6 with biphasic T waves in V5 and V6; there is no ST elevation in any territory and no posterior or right-sided changes on additional leads. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 78 ng/L (reference <14) drawn at 1 hour after symptom resolution. There is no pulmonary oedema clinically or radiographically and no haemodynamic instability. The cardiology registrar has calculated a GRACE score of 158.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1 mm V4 to V6, biphasic T waves V5 to V6. No ST elevation.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 78 ng/L (<14) at 1 h post-symptoms. eGFR 52. HbA1c 8.2%.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate timing of coronary angiography?

options.text: Within 24 hours of presentation, as part of an early invasive strategy
options.rationale: Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS recommends invasive angiography within 24 hours for high-risk NSTE-ACS: GRACE >140, dynamic ST or T-wave changes, or troponin rise consistent with MI. She meets all three criteria. The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for very-high-risk features (refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic/electrical instability, mechanical complication), which she does not have.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 2 hours of presentation, as part of an immediate invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is for haemodynamic instability, refractory ischaemia, life-threatening arrhythmia, or mechanical complications; none of these is present (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 72 hours of presentation, as part of a delayed invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 72 hour window applies to intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS without high-risk features; a GRACE of 158 with dynamic ECG changes and positive troponin places her in the high-risk group requiring intervention within 24 hours (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Selective invasive strategy guided by non-invasive ischaemia testing after discharge
options.rationale: A selective/ischaemia-guided approach is reserved for low-risk NSTE-ACS (GRACE <109, no dynamic changes, negative troponin), which does not apply here (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Defer angiography given CKD stage 3a; manage medically and reassess at 6 weeks
options.rationale: CKD stage 3a is not a contraindication to angiography; high-risk NSTE-ACS patients with CKD derive substantial benefit from early invasive management with contrast-minimisation and pre/post hydration (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; KHA-CARI).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Heart Foundation/CSANZ stratifies NSTE-ACS into four invasive timing categories: immediate (<2 h) for instability; early (<24 h) for high-risk; delayed (<72 h) for intermediate-risk; selective for low-risk. High-risk criteria include GRACE >140, dynamic ECG changes, or rise/fall of troponin meeting MI criteria.
key_points: GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTE-ACS, angio within 24 h
Refractory ischaemia, instability, mechanical complication = <2 h
Dynamic ECG and positive troponin push to early invasive
CKD is not a barrier; mitigate contrast risk instead
explanation.pearls: Treat 'NSTE-ACS' as a risk-stratified pathway not a single diagnosis; the timing decision is the assessable step (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 61-year-old man underwent successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD 6 weeks ago for anterior STEMI. He has been compliant with aspirin 100 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no atrial fibrillation, no prior bleeding, no prior stroke, and an eGFR of 78. He attends a routine cardiology review and asks how long he should remain on the two antiplatelets together. He has private health insurance, lives independently, and has no planned non-cardiac surgery. He has had no chest pain since the procedure and has commenced cardiac rehabilitation.

data_table.Procedure: Primary PCI LAD, 1 DES, 6 weeks ago.
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg, ticagrelor 90 mg BD, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metoprolol 50 mg BD.
data_table.Risk factors: No AF, no prior bleed/stroke, eGFR 78, no upcoming surgery.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate planned duration of dual antiplatelet therapy from the date of his PCI?

options.text: 1 month
options.rationale: 1 month DAPT is the abbreviated regimen for very high bleeding risk after DES, not standard post-ACS PCI; routine ACS PCI requires substantially longer DAPT (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: 12 months, then continue aspirin indefinitely
options.rationale: After ACS with PCI, Heart Foundation/CSANZ and Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular recommend DAPT (aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor, ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel) for 12 months in the absence of high bleeding risk, followed by lifelong aspirin monotherapy. Extension beyond 12 months may be considered for selected high ischaemic/low bleeding risk patients but is not standard.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: 6 months
options.rationale: 6 months DAPT applies to stable coronary disease PCI (non-ACS), not post-ACS; post-ACS requires 12 months by default (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: 3 months
options.rationale: 3 month DAPT is a shortened regimen for high bleeding risk in stable disease PCI; it is not the standard post-ACS duration (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Lifelong dual antiplatelet therapy with annual review
options.rationale: Lifelong DAPT is not standard; bleeding risk progressively outweighs ischaemic benefit beyond 12 months in most patients (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Default DAPT post-ACS PCI is 12 months (aspirin + ticagrelor preferred), then lifelong aspirin. Shorter durations (1, 3, 6 months) are tailored to bleeding risk or stable-disease PCI; extension beyond 12 months requires individualised risk/benefit (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
key_points: Post-ACS PCI: 12 months DAPT then aspirin lifelong
Ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel in ACS (not with lysis)
Shorter DAPT considered when bleeding risk is high (e.g., HAS-BLED, prior bleed, frailty)
Stable CAD PCI default is 6 months DAPT
explanation.pearls: Remember the indication (ACS vs stable) drives DAPT duration, not the stent type (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 67-year-old woman attends her regular general practice appointment for review of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation, which was identified on a 24 hour Holter monitor ordered for intermittent palpitations over the preceding 3 months. She has longstanding essential hypertension treated with perindopril/indapamide combination, and a past ischaemic stroke versus transient ischaemic attack 3 years ago with complete neurological recovery and no residual deficit on subsequent imaging. She has no diabetes mellitus, no heart failure, no peripheral or coronary vascular disease, normal renal function (eGFR 78, creatinine 64 micromol/L) and no prior major or minor bleeding episodes. Her current medications are perindopril/indapamide 5/1.25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, and paracetamol as required for occasional knee osteoarthritis. Blood pressure today is 132/80, HR 78 irregularly irregular, weight 72 kg. Her HAS-BLED score is 1 (hypertension alone, treated). She prefers an oral medication strategy without the need for routine INR blood monitoring and is keen on a once or twice daily regimen.

data_table.Risk factors: Age 67, female, HTN treated, prior TIA, no DM, no HF, no vascular disease.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril/indapamide 5/1.25 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol PRN.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate stroke-prevention recommendation?

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; it has minimal protective effect and similar bleeding risk to anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ 2018 AF Guideline).

options.text: No antithrombotic therapy; lifestyle modification only
options.rationale: She has CHA2DS2-VASc of at least 4 (age 65 to 74 = 1, female = 1, HTN = 1, prior TIA = 2), well above the threshold for anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Warfarin with INR target 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin is effective but DOACs are preferred first-line for non-valvular AF in patients without mechanical valves or moderate-severe mitral stenosis, especially when the patient prefers monitoring-free therapy (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as the standard non-valvular AF dose, with annual renal function review and reassessment of bleeding risk at each clinical contact
options.rationale: Her CHA2DS2-VASc is 4 (age 65 to 74 = 1, female = 1, HTN = 1, prior TIA = 2), exceeding the threshold of >=2 in women (>=1 in men, excluding female sex alone) for anticoagulation under NHFA/CSANZ 2018. DOACs are first-line over warfarin in non-valvular AF. She has no dose-reduction criteria (age >=80, weight <=60 kg, or creatinine >=133 micromol/L: needs >=2 of these), so the standard 5 mg twice daily dose applies (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel as a long-term stroke-prevention strategy in lieu of oral anticoagulation
options.rationale: DAPT is inferior to anticoagulation for AF stroke prevention and carries comparable bleeding risk; it is not recommended (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: Non-valvular AF stroke prevention: calculate CHA2DS2-VASc, anticoagulate when >=1 in men or >=2 in women (excluding sex). DOACs are first-line over warfarin. Apixaban dose reduction (2.5 mg BD) requires at least 2 of: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VASc: CHF, HTN, Age >=75 (2), DM, Stroke/TIA (2), Vasc disease, Age 65 to 74, female
Anticoagulate AF if >=1 (men) or >=2 (women, excluding sex)
DOACs preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF
Aspirin and DAPT are not stroke-prevention strategies in AF
explanation.pearls: Female sex contributes a point but is not an independent indication for anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

subtopic_detail: Rate vs rhythm control in AF with heart failure
stem: A 58-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 32% on TTE 4 weeks ago) and persistent atrial fibrillation diagnosed 7 months ago is reviewed in the dedicated heart failure clinic of a metropolitan tertiary hospital. He is on sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily and apixaban 5 mg twice daily, all at maximum tolerated doses with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records. Despite these guideline-directed therapies, he reports NYHA class III symptoms with breathlessness when flat-walking 50 metres on level ground and orthopnoea requiring two pillows at night, with one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea in the past week. Resting heart rate is 78 in AF on the clinic ECG; 6 minute walk test today is 280 metres (down from 360 m at his last review 3 months ago). He had one direct current cardioversion 4 months ago, which restored sinus rhythm initially with relapse to AF within 6 weeks despite continuation of beta-blocker therapy. He has no significant comorbidities (no diabetes, no CKD, no prior stroke) and lives 30 minutes from a metropolitan electrophysiology centre with established AF ablation programme. Symptoms have not improved despite optimal heart failure medical therapy for 3 months and adequate rate control.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 32%, mild LA dilatation, no significant valve disease.
data_table.Medications: Sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 BD, bisoprolol 7.5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, dapagliflozin 10 mg, apixaban 5 mg BD.
data_table.Functional: NYHA III, 6MWT 280 m, resting HR 78 in AF, BP 118/72.
data_table.Procedural history: DCCV 4 months ago, AF relapse at 6 weeks.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to improve symptoms and outcomes?

options.text: Increase bisoprolol to 10 mg daily for tighter rate control and continue current management
options.rationale: Rate is already controlled at 78 with adequate beta-blockade; symptoms persist despite GDMT and rate control. Tighter rate control without addressing rhythm has not been shown to improve outcomes in HFrEF (NHFA/CSANZ AF; CASTLE-AF; CASTLE-HTx).

options.text: Add amiodarone 200 mg daily after loading, for pharmacological rhythm control
options.rationale: Amiodarone is an option in HFrEF where flecainide and sotalol are contraindicated, but catheter ablation has superior outcomes (symptoms, mortality, HF hospitalisation) to amiodarone in HFrEF + AF and is preferred when feasible (CASTLE-AF; NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Repeat DCCV and load amiodarone for maintenance
options.rationale: Repeat DCCV without addressing the underlying substrate (and after early relapse) is unlikely to give durable rhythm control; in HFrEF, catheter ablation outperforms drug-based rhythm strategies (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: AV node ablation with biventricular pacing
options.rationale: AV node ablation plus pacing is a fallback for refractory rate-control failure; it is not first-line when catheter ablation of AF itself is feasible, as it commits the patient to lifelong pacing without restoring sinus rhythm (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for catheter ablation of atrial fibrillation as the rhythm-control strategy of choice in his HFrEF
options.rationale: In symptomatic AF with HFrEF despite optimal GDMT and adequate rate control, catheter ablation reduces all-cause mortality and HF hospitalisation and improves LVEF, symptoms and quality of life (CASTLE-AF, CABANA HF subgroup, CASTLE-HTx). NHFA/CSANZ recommends catheter ablation for symptomatic AF where rhythm control is desired and is the preferred rhythm strategy in HFrEF over antiarrhythmic drugs. This patient has failed DCCV, has ongoing NYHA III symptoms, and is geographically accessible to an EP centre.

explanation.summary: In symptomatic AF + HFrEF despite optimal GDMT and rate control, catheter ablation is the preferred rhythm strategy (mortality and HF hospitalisation benefit). Amiodarone is reserved for patients who are not ablation candidates. AV node ablation + pacing is a salvage option only.
key_points: HFrEF + symptomatic AF: catheter ablation > drug rhythm control (CASTLE-AF)
Optimise GDMT before declaring rhythm-control failure
Flecainide and sotalol are contraindicated in HFrEF
AV node ablation + CRT is salvage, not first-line
explanation.pearls: When AF is the perpetuating driver of HFrEF, restoring sinus rhythm can reverse the cardiomyopathy (NHFA/CSANZ).

stem: A 79-year-old man with persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation diagnosed 4 years ago has worsening chronic kidney disease with a current eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD-EPI), serum creatinine 218 micromol/L, and stable weight of 64 kg (BMI 22.4). His comorbidities include hypertension on perindopril, type 2 diabetes mellitus on basal insulin (HbA1c 7.1%), and a prior ischaemic stroke 2 years ago with mild residual right hemiparesis but functional independence. His calculated CHA2DS2-VASc score is 6 (age >=75 = 2, HTN = 1, DM = 1, prior CVA = 2) and HAS-BLED score is 3 (age >65, HTN, abnormal renal function). He has been on rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for 3 years, but his renal function has progressively declined over the past 6 months from an eGFR of 38 to the current 22. He is not yet on dialysis and his treating nephrologist estimates he is 12 to 18 months from requiring renal replacement therapy. He has no mechanical heart valve and no moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis on his last echocardiogram. He has had one prior epistaxis requiring nasal packing 18 months ago but no other bleeding. He lives independently with his wife in suburban Melbourne and is keen to remain on anticoagulation given his high stroke risk. He has no active malignancy and his platelet count, liver function tests and coagulation profile are normal.

data_table.Other: No mechanical valve, no significant MS, no active malignancy.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily requires CrCl >50 mL/min; for CrCl 15 to 49 the AMH dose is 15 mg daily, and the AMH advises caution for CrCl <30. Continuing 20 mg is inappropriate.

options.text: Switch to apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily with periodic renal function monitoring
options.rationale: Apixaban is the DOAC with the most renal-impairment evidence and has the lowest renal clearance (~27%). He meets the apixaban dose-reduction criteria: at least two of age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L - he has age 79 (not quite >=80) and creatinine 218 micromol/L (yes). However the AMH/PI also reduces dose to 2.5 mg BD when eGFR is 15 to 29 regardless of the standard criteria. The NHFA/CSANZ 2018 AF Guideline supports apixaban as the preferred DOAC in advanced CKD over warfarin given lower bleeding and similar efficacy.

options.text: Switch to dabigatran 110 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Dabigatran is ~80% renally cleared and is contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min in Australia (AMH). With eGFR 22, dabigatran is contraindicated.

options.text: Switch to warfarin with INR target 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin is acceptable in advanced CKD but DOACs (apixaban) are now preferred where eGFR is >=15 due to lower intracranial haemorrhage risk and comparable efficacy; warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves, significant mitral stenosis, dialysis or DOAC intolerance/contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation and refer for left atrial appendage occlusion
options.rationale: LAAO is reserved for patients with absolute contraindication to long-term anticoagulation, which he does not have; he tolerates anticoagulation and only needs dose/agent adjustment (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: DOAC selection in CKD is driven by renal clearance: dabigatran (~80%) > rivaroxaban (~35%) > edoxaban (~50%) > apixaban (~27%). Dabigatran is contraindicated below CrCl 30; rivaroxaban dose-reduces in CKD 3 to 4; apixaban is preferred in advanced CKD with the 2.5 mg BD dose criteria.
key_points: Dabigatran contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min
Rivaroxaban 15 mg daily for CrCl 15 to 49
Apixaban 2.5 mg BD if any 2 of: age >=80, wt <=60 kg, Cr >=133 micromol/L
Warfarin only for mechanical valves, significant MS, dialysis, or DOAC intolerance
explanation.pearls: Apixaban has the lowest renal clearance and best evidence in advanced CKD - the right DOAC for declining eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

subtopic_detail: HFrEF - four pillars guideline-directed medical therapy
stem: A 62-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (LVEF 28% on transthoracic echocardiogram performed during admission) presents to the heart failure clinic at a metropolitan tertiary centre 2 weeks after discharge from his index admission with acute pulmonary oedema. The admission echocardiogram showed global hypokinesis without focal wall motion abnormality, mild left atrial dilatation and no significant valvular disease. Cardiac MRI during admission excluded an infiltrative cardiomyopathy and showed no late gadolinium enhancement suggestive of myocarditis. Coronary angiography demonstrated non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He is in sinus rhythm. Current medications post-discharge are ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and frusemide 40 mg daily. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.4%, on metformin 1 g twice daily), no atrial fibrillation, no significant valve disease, eGFR 64, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, BP 122/76 supine and 118/74 standing, HR 64 sinus rhythm, and NYHA class II symptoms when walking up two flights of stairs. He has no history of angio-oedema, no symptomatic hypotension or dizziness, and no recent gout flare. His weight is 88 kg (down 4 kg since admission with diuresis) and he is keen to optimise medical therapy following written education from the heart failure nurse practitioner.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 28%, no significant valve disease, mild LA dilatation.
data_table.Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 64 sinus rhythm, NYHA II.
data_table.Current Rx: Ramipril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metformin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition or modification to his heart failure regimen?

options.text: Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan and add spironolactone 12.5 to 25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily over subsequent visits
options.rationale: NHFA/CSANZ 2018 (with 2022 update) recommends the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi if ARNI not tolerated/available), evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor. Each agent independently reduces mortality in HFrEF. Switch ACEi to ARNI (washout 36 hours), add MRA when K <5.0 and eGFR >30, and add SGLT2i regardless of diabetes status. Bisoprolol can be uptitrated. This sequenced approach matches the current quadruple therapy paradigm.

options.text: Add digoxin 125 micrograms daily for symptom relief
options.rationale: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is reserved for persistent symptoms despite the four pillars; it is not part of foundational GDMT (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with resting HR >=70 despite maximum tolerated beta-blocker; he is at HR 64 and has not yet had bisoprolol uptitrated, and lacks the four pillars (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add metolazone 2.5 mg as required
options.rationale: Diuretics treat congestion but do not modify mortality; he is euvolaemic on current dose and the priority is mortality-reducing GDMT, not diuretic escalation (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer immediately for cardiac resynchronisation therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) without further uptitration of oral guideline-directed medical therapy
options.rationale: Device therapy is considered after >=3 months of optimal medical therapy with persistent LVEF <=35% and appropriate QRS criteria; he has only been on partial therapy for 2 weeks (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: The four pillars of HFrEF (LVEF <=40%) GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), evidence-based beta-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol-XL, nebivolol), MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Each reduces mortality; aim to initiate all four within weeks then uptitrate.
key_points: ARNI > ACEi when tolerated; 36 h washout from ACEi
MRA needs K <5.0 and eGFR >30
SGLT2i used regardless of diabetes status
Devices (ICD/CRT) considered only after >=3 months optimal GDMT
explanation.pearls: Sequence matters less than getting all four pillars on board within weeks at low doses, then uptitrate (NHFA/CSANZ 2022 update).

subtopic_detail: HFpEF - SGLT2 inhibitor as first-line disease-modifying therapy
stem: A 73-year-old woman presents to a community cardiology clinic with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 6 months, now limiting her ability to walk to her letterbox without stopping. She has longstanding essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.6%, on metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg daily), obesity (BMI 34, weight 92 kg), and moderate obstructive sleep apnoea well-controlled on nightly CPAP at 9 cmH2O with good compliance documented. She had a hospital admission 3 months ago for fluid overload requiring intravenous diuresis and was discharged on oral frusemide as needed. Her examination today shows BP 138/82, HR 76 sinus, JVP not elevated, mild bibasal end-inspiratory crackles, and no peripheral oedema. Transthoracic echocardiogram performed last week shows preserved LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, left atrial volume index 38 mL/m2 (dilated), septal e' velocity 6 cm/s, E/e' ratio of 14, and no significant valvular disease. NT-proBNP is 612 pg/mL (elevated for her age and BMI, after BMI correction). Her current medications are perindopril 10 mg daily, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. Renal function: eGFR 58, K 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. There is no history of gout, recurrent urinary infection, or genital fungal infection. She is keen on therapies that will reduce hospitalisation and would prefer to avoid additional injectable medications.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, LAVI 38, E/e' 14.
data_table.Current Rx: Perindopril 10, indapamide 2.5, metformin, gliclazide MR, atorvastatin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate addition to her management for heart failure outcomes?

options.text: Add sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily
options.rationale: PARAGON-HF did not meet its primary endpoint in HFpEF; sacubitril/valsartan has a Class IIb recommendation only and SGLT2 inhibitors have stronger evidence and a Class I-equivalent role in current NHFA/CSANZ HF guideline updates for HFpEF.

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily
options.rationale: TOPCAT showed a modest benefit only in regional subgroups; spironolactone can be considered in HFpEF but is not first-line, and SGLT2 inhibitors have clearer mortality/hospitalisation benefit (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER).

options.text: Add bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
options.rationale: Beta-blockers do not have robust mortality benefit in HFpEF and may worsen chronotropic incompetence; not first-line for HFpEF unless required for another indication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add empagliflozin 10 mg daily as first-line disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF
options.rationale: EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin) and DELIVER (dapagliflozin) both demonstrated reduction in the composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFpEF, driven primarily by reduction in HF hospitalisation. NHFA/CSANZ 2022 guideline update recommends SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line disease-modifying therapy in HFpEF irrespective of diabetes status. She also has type 2 diabetes, providing dual benefit. eGFR 58 is well above the threshold for initiation.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

options.text: Increase frusemide as required and emphasise weight loss and CPAP adherence only
options.rationale: Lifestyle (weight loss, CPAP, BP control) is essential adjunct, but withholding disease-modifying therapy (SGLT2 inhibitor) misses a clear mortality/hospitalisation benefit (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: HFpEF (LVEF >=50%) management: BP control, comorbidity treatment (sleep apnoea, obesity, diabetes), diuretics for congestion. SGLT2 inhibitors are first-line disease-modifying therapy with Class I evidence based on EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER. ARNI and MRA have a more limited role.
key_points: EMPEROR-Preserved + DELIVER: SGLT2i reduce HF hospitalisation in HFpEF
SGLT2i benefit is independent of diabetes status
ARNI (PARAGON-HF) and MRA (TOPCAT) have weaker HFpEF evidence
Aggressively manage comorbidities: HTN, AF, obesity, OSA, CKD
explanation.pearls: The HFpEF treatment landscape changed dramatically post-2021; the SGLT2 inhibitor is no longer optional in suitable HFpEF patients (NHFA/CSANZ).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

subtopic_detail: Acute pulmonary oedema - early NIV and IV nitrate
stem: A 78-year-old woman with known HFrEF (LVEF 30% on her most recent echocardiogram 4 months ago) and a background of long-standing hypertension, permanent atrial fibrillation rate-controlled on bisoprolol and apixaban, and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline eGFR 48) is brought to a busy metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance with severe dyspnoea. She has been progressively breathless over the previous 24 hours after dietary indiscretion at a family birthday and woke with acute orthopnoea 2 hours ago. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is sitting upright bolt-upright, gasping, with marked accessory muscle use, tracheal tug, and visible pink frothy sputum at the lips. Vital signs at triage: HR 132 in atrial fibrillation, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on 15 L non-rebreather mask, afebrile at 36.6 degrees Celsius. Examination shows JVP elevated to the angle of the jaw, widespread bilateral fine inspiratory crackles to the mid-zones, gallop rhythm with audible S3, and cool peripheries with capillary refill of 3 seconds. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate 130 to 140 and no acute ischaemic changes. Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates with Kerley B lines and bilateral pleural effusions. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 38 ng/L (<14), creatinine 142 micromol/L (up from baseline 110), potassium 4.6, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. She is not opioid-naive at home (uses regular tramadol for chronic back pain) and has no fixed obstruction valve lesion. She has a current Resuscitation Plan documenting a wish for full active treatment short of invasive intubation if it can be avoided.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 AF, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on NRB 15 L, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, pink frothy sputum, JVP to jaw, widespread crackles, gallop, cool peripheries CRT 3 s.
data_table.ECG: AF rate 130 to 140, no ST changes.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 38 ng/L, Cr 142, K 4.6, lactate 2.4. CXR: bilateral alveolar oedema, Kerley B.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management combination?

options.text: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus, IV morphine 5 mg for anxiolysis and venodilation, with low-flow oxygen at 4 L via nasal prongs
options.rationale: Morphine in acute heart failure is associated with increased mortality and ICU admission; routine use is not recommended. Low-flow nasal prongs cannot deliver adequate FiO2 for SpO2 86% on NRB (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Intubate immediately for invasive mechanical ventilation
options.rationale: She has a Resuscitation Plan favouring avoidance of intubation if possible; non-invasive ventilation should be trialled first and very often averts intubation in acute pulmonary oedema (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: IV digoxin 500 micrograms loading and IV metoprolol 5 mg for rate control
options.rationale: IV beta-blocker in decompensated HF with cool peripheries and elevated lactate risks haemodynamic collapse; rate control is secondary to afterload reduction and ventilation in hypertensive APO (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence IV nesiritide and IV dobutamine infusion
options.rationale: Nesiritide is not available in Australia, and inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension; this patient is hypertensive (BP 192/108) and needs afterload reduction, not inotropy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence BiPAP (e.g., IPAP 12 to 14, EPAP 6 to 8 cmH2O), IV GTN infusion titrated to BP, and IV frusemide 40 mg bolus
options.rationale: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema is afterload-mediated; the priorities are oxygenation/ventilation and afterload reduction. Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP) reduces intubation rates and improves dyspnoea and oxygenation (3CPO trial; NHFA/CSANZ). IV nitrate is the afterload reducer of choice in hypertensive APO (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular). IV loop diuretic addresses congestion. The combination addresses oxygenation, preload, and afterload simultaneously. Avoid morphine.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

explanation.summary: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema management: sit up, high-flow oxygen, NIV (CPAP or BiPAP) early, IV nitrate for afterload reduction, IV loop diuretic for congestion. Avoid morphine. Inotropes only for cardiogenic shock. Identify and treat the precipitant (AF, ACS, infection, non-compliance).
key_points: NIV reduces intubation in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (3CPO)
GTN infusion is the afterload reducer in hypertensive APO
Loop diuretic addresses congestion; do not over-diurese into shock
Avoid morphine - higher mortality and ICU admission
explanation.pearls: BP 192/108 with APO = afterload problem; reduce afterload before chasing diuresis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

subtopic_detail: Cardiac vs vasovagal syncope - high-risk features
stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to ED after collapsing without warning while shopping. Bystanders report he became briefly stiff with a few jerks of the arms; he was unresponsive for around 30 seconds and recovered immediately on the floor with no confusion. He recalls no nausea, no warm or sweaty feeling, and no pre-syncopal symptoms. He has had two similar episodes in the last 2 months, one while sitting watching television. Examination shows BP 142/82 lying, 140/82 standing, HR 60 sinus, no postural drop. Cardiovascular and neurological examinations are unremarkable. ECG shows sinus rhythm at 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, with bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block). Bloods including FBC, UEC, glucose, troponin, and TSH are within reference. He drives a private car daily.

data_table.ECG: Sinus 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB + LAFB (bifascicular block).
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, glucose, hs-troponin, TSH all within reference.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for presumed vasovagal syncope
options.rationale: His episodes lack prodrome, occur while sitting, and his ECG shows bifascicular block in an older man with vascular risk; these are high-risk features for cardiac syncope, not vasovagal (BMJ Best Practice; ESC Syncope).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Arrange outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected seizure
options.rationale: Brief tonic-clonic jerks (convulsive syncope) are common in any cause of cerebral hypoperfusion and do not indicate seizure; rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion argues against seizure. Cardiac evaluation takes priority (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Admit for cardiac monitoring, transthoracic echocardiogram, and inpatient cardiology review; advise not to drive until cleared
options.rationale: Red flags for cardiac syncope are present: age >60, syncope while sitting/supine, absent prodrome, abnormal ECG with bifascicular block, and recurrent episodes. ESC 2018 Syncope and BMJ Best Practice mandate admission with cardiac monitoring and echocardiography. He must not drive: Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 imposes a non-driving period (4 weeks for single episode without high-risk features; longer for recurrent or unexplained cardiac syncope until cause identified and treated).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Arrange outpatient 24-hour Holter monitor and tilt-table test
options.rationale: Holter and tilt are appropriate later workup but outpatient deferral is unsafe given his high-risk features; admission with monitoring is required (ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Commence midodrine 2.5 mg three times daily for orthostatic intolerance
options.rationale: He has no orthostatic drop and no orthostatic intolerance; midodrine is inappropriate and risks worsening hypertension (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

explanation.summary: Cardiac syncope red flags: age >60, structural heart disease, exertional syncope, syncope while supine/sitting, absent prodrome, family history of sudden death, abnormal ECG (bifascicular block, prolonged QT, Brugada pattern). These mandate admission with telemetry and TTE. Convulsive syncope (brief jerks during cerebral hypoperfusion) does not indicate epilepsy.
key_points: Cardiac syncope: abrupt, no prodrome, supine/exertional, abnormal ECG
Vasovagal: prodrome (warmth, nausea, blurred vision), upright trigger
Bifascicular block + syncope suggests intermittent CHB until proven otherwise
Austroads imposes non-driving periods after cardiac/unexplained syncope
explanation.pearls: Always ask about driving and apply Austroads non-driving periods (BMJ Best Practice; Austroads 2022).

sources.label: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

subtopic_detail: Brugada syndrome - type 1 ECG with syncope
stem: A 34-year-old male software engineer of South-East Asian background is brought to a tertiary hospital emergency department after a single witnessed episode of syncope at 0300 hours while sleeping next to his wife. His wife reports that he became briefly stiff for around 10 seconds with gasping agonal-type respirations and then recovered spontaneously over 1 minute with no confusion or post-event lethargy. He drinks approximately 4 standard drinks twice weekly, takes no regular medications, and has had a non-specific febrile illness over the previous 48 hours with paracetamol use for symptomatic relief of myalgia. He has had no previous episodes of syncope, pre-syncope, or palpitations. Family history is notable: a paternal first cousin died suddenly at age 28 during sleep with no clear cause identified at coronial autopsy; his father is well at age 62 with no cardiac history. Examination today is unremarkable; he is fully oriented with no neurological deficit. BP 124/72, HR 64 sinus, RR 14, T 37.8 degrees Celsius (febrile), SpO2 99% room air. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates a coved-type ST elevation of 2.5 mm in V1 and V2 with negative T waves and a J point >=2 mm, type 1 pattern present in two right precordial leads, persisting on repeat ECG with leads in standard and high (second intercostal space) positions. The QTc interval is 410 ms (normal). Cardiac troponin, electrolytes, magnesium and TSH are all within reference range. Echocardiogram is structurally normal with no regional wall motion abnormality. Urine toxicology screen is negative. There is no history of cocaine, tricyclic antidepressant, or sodium channel blocker exposure. He plans to return to work in 4 days but the cardiology team is reviewing him in the chest pain unit for definitive management.

data_table.ECG: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation >=2 mm with negative T in V1 and V2. QTc 410 ms.
data_table.Family Hx: Paternal cousin sudden death age 28 during sleep.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin, electrolytes, Mg, TSH normal. Echo structurally normal. Tox screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol for fever, and arrange outpatient cardiology review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: He has spontaneous type 1 Brugada pattern AND a syncopal episode of likely arrhythmic origin (sleep, brief, no prodrome) plus family history of sudden death; this is the highest-risk group for sudden cardiac death and requires definitive therapy, not outpatient deferral (CSANZ/HRS Brugada statement; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Refer for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) implantation under the symptomatic Brugada syndrome Class I indication
options.rationale: Symptomatic Brugada syndrome with spontaneous type 1 ECG and unexplained syncope likely arrhythmic in origin carries a Class I indication for ICD per the HRS/EHRA/APHRS expert consensus (endorsed by CSANZ) and is reflected in BMJ Best Practice. Family history of sudden death adds to risk. Fever (which unmasks/exacerbates Brugada) should be aggressively treated with paracetamol as adjunct, but does not obviate the indication for definitive therapy. Quinidine and EP-guided VT ablation are alternatives in some scenarios but ICD remains primary.
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence flecainide 100 mg twice daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Flecainide is a sodium channel blocker that UNMASKS and worsens Brugada (used as a provocative challenge test); it would precipitate ventricular arrhythmia. Class IC agents are contraindicated (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence amiodarone 200 mg daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Amiodarone has no proven benefit in Brugada and does not replace an ICD for proven symptomatic disease; quinidine is the antiarrhythmic with the strongest Brugada-specific evidence as ICD adjunct, but ICD remains primary (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Arrange genetic testing for SCN5A and other Brugada-associated variants, and await the result before deciding on further definitive management
options.rationale: Genetic testing informs cascade screening of relatives but a negative SCN5A does not exclude Brugada (only ~20-30% of probands have identified pathogenic variants) and does not change management of a clinically diagnosed symptomatic patient. Delaying ICD to await genetics is unsafe (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

explanation.summary: Brugada syndrome diagnosis requires spontaneous type 1 ECG (coved ST elevation >=2 mm in >=1 right precordial lead V1 or V2) plus a clinical feature (documented VF/polymorphic VT, syncope of probable arrhythmic origin, nocturnal agonal respiration, family history of sudden death <45, type 1 ECG in family member). Symptomatic Brugada has Class I ICD indication. Class IC drugs and fever provoke the syndrome.
key_points: Type 1 Brugada pattern + syncope = ICD (Class I)
Avoid sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone) and TCAs
Fever unmasks Brugada - treat aggressively with paracetamol
Quinidine is the antiarrhythmic of choice as ICD adjunct or bridge
explanation.pearls: Look for spontaneous (not just drug-induced) type 1 pattern in any young adult with nocturnal syncope or sudden death family history (CSANZ/HRS).

sources.label: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

stem: A 46-year-old woman is referred to a metropolitan hypertension clinic with progressively worsening hypertension that has resisted intensification over 18 months. Her home blood pressure log, taken with a validated upper-arm device twice daily, averages 162/96 mmHg despite perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg taken daily with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records over the past 12 months. She has no obstructive sleep apnoea symptoms (no snoring, no witnessed apnoeas, Epworth 4), no flushing or palpitations, no abdominal bruit on examination, and no family history of phaeochromocytoma, paraganglioma or thyroid medullary carcinoma. She has had intermittent hypokalaemia (lowest documented potassium 3.1 mmol/L) attributed by her referring GP to indapamide. Her most recent UEC shows: Na 142, K 3.4, Cl 102, HCO3 28 mmol/L, urea 5.6, creatinine 72 micromol/L, eGFR 89. Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.7%. TSH and free T4 within reference range. Urinary catecholamine and metanephrine screen normal on two separate 24-hour collections. Renal artery Doppler ultrasonography shows no evidence of renal artery stenosis bilaterally. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon positive). She is keen to investigate a potentially reversible secondary cause given her young age and target-organ damage on ECG.

data_table.BP: Home average 162/96 on perindopril 10, amlodipine 10, indapamide 2.5.
data_table.Bloods: Na 142, K 3.4 (low end), HCO3 28, Cr 72, eGFR 89, HbA1c 5.7%, TSH normal.
data_table.Other workup: Urinary catecholamines normal, renal Doppler no RAS, ECG LVH.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: 24-hour urinary metanephrines (repeat)
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma screening has already been done with normal urinary catecholamines and she has no suggestive symptoms; repeating is low-yield (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: MR angiography of renal arteries
options.rationale: Renovascular hypertension has been excluded by renal Doppler in a patient without other suggestive features (no abdominal bruit, no flash pulmonary oedema, no ACEi-induced AKI); MRA is not the next step (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: Cushing screening is reasonable when there are clinical features of hypercortisolism (central obesity, striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy, glucose intolerance); none are described and her HbA1c is normal. ARR has higher pre-test probability given hypokalaemia and resistant HTN (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio after appropriate medication adjustment
options.rationale: Primary aldosteronism prevalence in resistant hypertension is 15 to 20% and is the most common reversible cause. The Endocrine Society and BMJ Best Practice recommend ARR screening in resistant HTN, spontaneous or diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN with adrenal incidentaloma, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Pre-test preparation: replace potassium, normalise sodium, and ideally substitute interfering drugs (MRAs stopped for 4 to 6 weeks; ACEi/ARB/diuretics may falsely lower ratio but ARR is generally still interpretable with knowledge of medications). She has resistant HTN and diuretic-associated hypokalaemia: a textbook ARR indication.
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg empirically and reassess BP at 12 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical spironolactone is an appropriate fourth-line add-on in resistant HTN, but starting it before ARR screening will suppress the test for at least 4 to 6 weeks and miss the chance to diagnose primary aldosteronism (which would alter management and offer potential cure via adrenalectomy) (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

explanation.summary: Screen for primary aldosteronism (ARR) in resistant HTN, spontaneous/diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN + adrenal incidentaloma, HTN with family history of early CVA/PA, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Confirm with saline suppression or fludrocortisone suppression, then adrenal CT and adrenal vein sampling if surgery considered.
key_points: PA causes 15 to 20% of resistant HTN
Screen with morning ARR after K replacement
Stop MRAs 4 to 6 weeks before testing
Lateralised disease (Conn adenoma) is surgically curable
explanation.pearls: Resistant HTN + low K = Conn's until proven otherwise; do the ARR before adding spironolactone (Endocrine Society).

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

subtopic_detail: NHFA hypertension - lifestyle as first-line for grade 1 low-risk
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his GP for a workplace medical. He is asymptomatic, exercises twice a week, drinks 8 standard drinks per week, has BMI 28, does not smoke, has no diabetes, and has no family history of premature cardiovascular disease. Three separate clinic BP readings over 6 weeks average 146/92. Home BP averaged over 7 days is 142/88. Bloods: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, triglycerides 1.2, fasting glucose 5.1, eGFR >90, normal UEC and urine ACR. ECG normal. His calculated Australian absolute 5-year CV risk (NVDPA) is 3%.

data_table.Bloods: Chol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, TG 1.2, FBG 5.1, eGFR >90, ACR normal.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Lifestyle modification (reduce alcohol to <=10 standard drinks/week, increase aerobic exercise to >=30 minutes most days, DASH-style diet, weight reduction, salt <5 g/day) with reassessment in 3 to 6 months
options.rationale: NHFA 2016 (and subsequent updates) recommends lifestyle modification as first-line therapy for grade 1 hypertension (140 to 159/90 to 99) in patients at low absolute CV risk (<10% 5-year risk). Pharmacological therapy is added if BP remains uncontrolled at 3 to 6 months despite adherent lifestyle change, or earlier if absolute risk is moderate/high. His CV risk is 3% and his BP is mildly elevated, fitting first-line lifestyle.
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Commence perindopril 5 mg daily immediately as first-line pharmacotherapy, with home BP review and dose titration at 6-week intervals to target
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is added when low-risk grade 1 HTN fails to respond to lifestyle, or immediately when risk is moderate/high; not first-line for this low-risk patient (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Commence amlodipine 5 mg daily immediately as a single-agent dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, with review at 4 weeks for tolerance and BP response
options.rationale: Same reasoning - lifestyle is first-line in low-risk grade 1 HTN (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Refer for 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring to exclude white-coat hypertension before offering any intervention or lifestyle counselling, deferring further management until the result is available at 2 weeks
options.rationale: ABPM is useful to exclude white-coat HTN when clinic and home readings disagree; here clinic and home readings are concordant for hypertension. Lifestyle does not need to wait for ABPM (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Reassure that he is below any treatment threshold and review with repeat BP measurement in 12 months, without lifestyle counselling or further investigation
options.rationale: His BP meets criteria for grade 1 hypertension and warrants intervention (lifestyle), not 12-month deferral (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

explanation.summary: Grade 1 HTN (140 to 159/90 to 99) at low absolute CV risk: 3 to 6 months of lifestyle (DASH-style diet, salt <5 g/day, alcohol limits, exercise, weight reduction) then add pharmacotherapy if uncontrolled. Higher risk or higher BP grade triggers earlier or immediate medication.
key_points: Use absolute CV risk (NVDPA calculator) to guide pharmacotherapy threshold
DASH diet, exercise, weight loss, salt and alcohol limits
Reassess at 3 to 6 months for low-risk
Pharmacotherapy first-line: ACEi/ARB, dihydropyridine CCB, or thiazide
explanation.pearls: Absolute CV risk - not BP number alone - drives the decision to start drugs (NHFA 2016).

sources.label: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

subtopic_detail: Mobitz II AV block - indication for permanent pacemaker
stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidaemia is admitted to a regional hospital with two episodes of presyncope over 48 hours. She has no chest pain. Her medications are perindopril 5 mg and atorvastatin 40 mg; she takes no AV-blocking agents. On telemetry she has predominantly sinus rhythm at 62 bpm with intermittent dropped QRS complexes; the preceding PR intervals are constant at 200 ms before each non-conducted P wave. The QRS during conducted beats is 140 ms with a right bundle branch block morphology. Her BP is 132/76 between episodes and drops to 84/52 during prolonged pauses (longest documented pause 4.2 seconds). Echocardiogram is structurally normal with LVEF 60%. Bloods: TSH 2.1, electrolytes normal, troponin negative, no infection on screen, no Lyme exposure. She has no reversible cause identified (no recent cardiac surgery, no AV-blocking medications, no acute MI, no electrolyte derangement).

data_table.Telemetry: Sinus 62 bpm with intermittent dropped QRS; constant PR 200 ms preceding non-conducted P. Longest pause 4.2 s.
data_table.ECG: RBBB morphology QRS 140 ms during conducted beats.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation under Class I indication for symptomatic Mobitz II AV block
options.rationale: Mobitz II second-degree AV block (constant PR with sudden non-conducted P) is a Class I indication for permanent pacing regardless of symptoms because the block is below the AV node (His-Purkinje), unpredictable, and prone to progress to complete heart block with unreliable escape rhythm. Wide QRS (RBBB) supports infranodal block. ESC 2021 Pacing and CSANZ position support this indication. Reversible causes have been excluded.
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Atropine 600 microgram IV bolus and observe
options.rationale: Atropine acts on the AV node and frequently does NOT improve infranodal (Mobitz II, CHB with wide escape) block; it can paradoxically worsen it by speeding atrial rate without improving infranodal conduction. Atropine is a temporising measure, not definitive (ANZCOR ALS adult bradycardia algorithm).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Isoprenaline infusion and discharge with outpatient cardiology follow-up
options.rationale: Isoprenaline is a temporary inpatient bridge; discharge with this is unsafe and ignores the Class I pacemaker indication (ANZCOR; ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Reduce perindopril and observe with daily telemetry on the ward
options.rationale: Perindopril does not cause AV block; no reversible cause is identifiable and observation alone does not address the Class I indication for permanent pacing (ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: Electrophysiology study with potential AV node ablation as a definitive procedure to control her bradyarrhythmia and presyncope episodes
options.rationale: AV node ablation deliberately creates complete heart block and is used to control refractory tachyarrhythmias; it has no role in primary AV block management. EP study is occasionally used to localise block (especially in fascicular conduction disease with syncope), but the diagnosis here is clear from surface ECG (ESC).
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

explanation.summary: Permanent pacing is Class I indicated for: third-degree AV block, Mobitz II, advanced AV block (>=2:1), symptomatic sinus node dysfunction, alternating bundle branch block, and acquired complete heart block irrespective of symptoms. Mobitz I (Wenckebach) with narrow QRS is usually intra-nodal and does not need pacing unless symptomatic.
key_points: Mobitz I (narrow QRS) = AV nodal, usually no pacing unless symptomatic
Mobitz II = infra-nodal, Class I pacing regardless of symptoms
Wide QRS during AV block suggests infra-nodal pathology
Always exclude reversible causes: ischaemia, drugs, hyperkalaemia, hypothyroidism, Lyme
explanation.pearls: PR-constant-then-dropped = Mobitz II; PR-lengthening-then-dropped = Mobitz I (ESC 2021).

sources.label: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

subtopic_detail: Complete heart block with haemodynamic compromise - immediate management
stem: A 74-year-old man is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance after a witnessed syncopal episode at home. His wife reports that he stood from his armchair, became pale, and collapsed without warning, hitting his head lightly on the carpeted floor; he was unresponsive for approximately 90 seconds before recovering some awareness. On arrival to the resuscitation bay he is confused (GCS 13: E4 V3 M6), cool peripherally, pale and diaphoretic. Vital signs at triage: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, blood glucose 7.8 mmol/L on point-of-care testing. Capillary refill is prolonged at 4 seconds. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates complete atrioventricular dissociation with an atrial rate of 84 and a ventricular escape rhythm of 28 with broad QRS complexes (160 ms) and a left bundle branch block morphology consistent with an infra-nodal escape. There are no acute ischaemic ST changes and no Q waves. He has no known cardiac history. His regular medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and metformin 1 g twice daily. He has had no recent illness, no diarrhoea, no overdose (no recent prescription changes, no access to other medications), and no environmental exposures. Initial bloods drawn at arrival: troponin pending, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, glucose 7.8, urea and creatinine within normal limits, calcium and magnesium normal. The ANZCOR adult bradycardia algorithm is being followed by the ED senior. The on-call cardiology registrar has been telephoned and is 15 minutes away from the department. The patient remains in extremis with poor perfusion despite being supine and on high-flow oxygen.

data_table.Vitals: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Cool peripherally, CRT 4 s, pale, diaphoretic, confused.
data_table.ECG: Complete AV dissociation. Atrial rate 84, ventricular escape rate 28, broad QRS 160 ms (LBBB morphology). No acute ischaemic changes.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention while awaiting cardiology?

options.text: IV adrenaline 1 mg bolus stat and prepare for immediate rapid sequence intubation as the airway intervention takes priority over rate restoration
options.rationale: 1 mg adrenaline bolus is the cardiac arrest dose; for symptomatic bradycardia, ANZCOR recommends adrenaline INFUSION 2 to 10 micrograms/min titrated to response, or 20 to 50 microgram boluses. A 1 mg bolus risks hypertensive crisis and arrhythmia; intubation is not the immediate priority while perfusion can be restored with pacing.

options.text: Atropine 600 micrograms IV, repeated every 3 to 5 minutes to a maximum cumulative dose of 3 mg, as the sole pharmacological strategy while awaiting cardiology review
options.rationale: Atropine acts at the AV node and is generally INEFFECTIVE in complete heart block with a wide, infra-nodal escape rhythm; ANZCOR explicitly recommends moving directly to pacing or chronotropic infusion in such cases, although atropine can be tried briefly as a temporising step. Atropine alone is insufficient as the immediate intervention for this haemodynamic state.

options.text: Commence transcutaneous pacing with analgesia/sedation, set rate 70 to 80, increase output until electrical and mechanical capture confirmed, while arranging emergent transvenous pacing
options.rationale: ANZCOR Adult Bradycardia algorithm: in haemodynamically compromised bradycardia unresponsive to atropine or where atropine is unlikely to work (infra-nodal CHB with wide escape), transcutaneous pacing is first-line emergency intervention. Confirm BOTH electrical capture (pacing spike followed by QRS) AND mechanical capture (palpable pulse or arterial tracing). Provide adequate sedation/analgesia (TCP is painful). Arrange definitive transvenous pacing followed by permanent pacemaker. Atropine can be tried concurrently but TCP should not be delayed.
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: 1 L 0.9% saline bolus over 15 minutes to optimise preload and reassess perfusion and conscious state before considering rate-restoring measures
options.rationale: Fluid will not address the underlying conduction problem and may precipitate pulmonary oedema in an elderly patient with likely impaired LV reserve. Definitive treatment is restoring rate (ANZCOR).

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution given over 10 minutes empirically while awaiting biochemistry, with repeat ECG at 5 minutes
options.rationale: Calcium is for confirmed or suspected hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker toxicity; K is 4.4 mmol/L and amlodipine overdose is not suggested by history. Not the immediate intervention (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: ANZCOR adult bradycardia algorithm: if signs of life-threatening haemodynamic compromise, atropine 600 microgram IV (may repeat) as initial pharmacological step, but for wide-QRS infra-nodal block atropine often fails - escalate immediately to transcutaneous pacing (with sedation) or chronotropic infusion (adrenaline 2 to 10 micrograms/min, isoprenaline). Arrange transvenous pacing then permanent device.
key_points: Always confirm BOTH electrical and mechanical capture during TCP
Atropine often ineffective in wide-QRS infra-nodal block
Adrenaline infusion 2 to 10 mcg/min is an alternative when pacing unavailable
Identify and treat reversible causes (ischaemia, hyperkalaemia, drugs)
explanation.pearls: Mechanical capture (palpable pulse) is the goal, not just pacing spikes on the monitor (ANZCOR).

sources.label: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT
